utterances: Of course, our favorite film buff Murray Horwitz is here to campaign for his favorite.
Nice to have you back, Murray.
Senator McCain has proposed something he refers to as the Lexington Project.

It has become known as all of the above energy plan because it looks at our energy needs both in the near term, the middle - medium term, over the long term, and involves the use of all sorts of energy, quite frankly, fossil fuels, alternatives, renewables, nuclear power, innovations that we're going to need to move ourselves from a situation in which we are dangerously dependant on imported oil.
It exposes us to national security threats, economic security threats, and environmental security threats.
And I'd be happy to lay out any piece of it that you'd like.
Well, you mentioned fossil fuels.

That's certainly been a point of contention between Democrats and Republicans over the summer, with votes on drilling more or not drilling more because oil really can't be the long term energy solution.
That's the Democrats' position.
I think Senator McCain would agree.
He has been a staunch proponent of addressing global warming.

He has been someone who has introduced, along with Senator Lieberman, bipartisan legislation beginning in 2003 to have a cap and trade system that would provide powerful incentives to move away from carbon-based fossil fuels.
But any expert will tell you that over the foreseeable future, carbon and fossil fuels are an integral part of our energy future.

And the senator's belief is that we should do some things in the near term because we have an urgent need to not send money abroad to finance countries who don't show our values, in some cases finance the terrorists who are trying to harm us.
So that begins by more domestic production of oil and natural gas as domestic options as an alternative to imports.
How soon would Senator McCain think we might be deriving energy that we could use from increased domestic production, new drilling and that sort of thing?
The Department of Energy suggests that, if you drill in the outer continental shelf for oil, the fastest you can probably get that oil to market would be three and a half years.
Five is a number that I think they're more comfortable with.
It'll depend on the fuel source itself, but there's no excuse to not get going on this.
You know, the experts would also say we have to get going on other things as well.
The senator's proposing dramatic change in the way we drive.

To incentivize the use of cleaner cars, he has a tailpipe credit which you, if you want to think of- which is $5,000 if you buy a car that doesn't have any carbon dioxide coming out of the tail pipe, and a smaller credit for cars that are plug-in hybrids, things closer to a conventional automobile.
Let's do that, so that we start driving on things that are not oil.
Is that something that the senator might be prepared to, well, wait a while on because of our current fiscal crisis?
I think both campaigns have said, maybe we'd have to make adjustments.
What adjustments would Senator McCain make?
In the main, his proposals are not ones which involve big new spending programs.
The tailpipe credit is one that, obviously, he's suggested would be a quicker way to get us to drive differently, and he would review that proposal in light of the fiscal situation.
The senator does say that he wants 45 nuclear plants developed over the next 30 years, I think it is, and wants an international repository for spent uranium fuel for nuclear power plants.
Where would that international repository be?

Well, I think that's less to be thought of as a specific proposal than to recognize that, number one, around the globe, there are countries with commercial nuclear electricity industries that are more extensive than the United States, and they reprocess their spent fuel, thereby cutting back on the amount of waste that's needed to be stored.
They also have a shared desire to have a repository, and as a world community, we have a shared desire to make sure we don't have a proliferation problem.
The U.S.
could, on its own revisit, then-President Carter's decision to stop reprocessing spent fuel.
The senator thinks that's one part of addressing the storage problem for nuclear waste.
You know, other countries can do this.
We know that addressing global warming will require dramatic technical innovations.
This is a known technology right now that has zero emissions.
We should use it.
And in his formulation, if we've got a more extensive nuclear power grid, we can also be plugging cleaner cars into it and driving off those reactors.
We talk about energy independence in this country, many people would say that means.
let's get off Mideast oil.
The senator thinks we can do that how soon, 2025?
Most experts, perhaps all experts, would say that physical independence from imported energy is not a sensible goal.
The goal would be to have an energy portfolio in the United States which is more diversified than is the one right now.
And as a result, Americans don't get out of bed and find themselves hostage to big price movements in international oil markets.
They don't fear terrorist attacks on Saudi oil facilities.
We have so diversified and so reduced our dependence that even if there is some physical connection, it's not something that puts us at risk.
Douglas Holtz-Eakin, senior policy advisor to Senator John McCain's presidential campaign.
Thank you.
Thank you.
And we'll follow tomorrow with an interview with Senator Barack Obama's energy advisor.
This is NEWS & NOTES.
I'm Tony Cox.
All month long, we'll be exploring the dynamics of the family.
Black families, like many American families, are changing in part because they're choosing more and more to adopt children from other countries.
The Department of Homeland Security reports that Americans adopted more than 20,000 children from overseas last year.
White celebrities have gotten lots of attention for looking outside the U.S.
to expand their families.
But what do black families look for when they choose to adopt abroad?
And what's in store for a foreign child when he or she comes into a black American home?
Sherry Redwood is a Baltimore area entrepreneur and a black mother in the process of adopting a 15-month-old boy from Ethiopia.
She joins us.
And also joining us today is Thomas Atwood.
He is president and CEO of the National Council for Adoption.
Welcome, both of you.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Tom, let me begin with you.
The State Department reports that intercountry adoptions are on the rise in the United States.

Why do you think so many people are looking outside of this country to adopt especially, especially when there are still a lot of American children who need homes and families, and especially among that group, black children?
Well, Tony, there are both some myths and some realities that cause people to turn towards - internationally for adoption.
If you think about adopting an infant domestically, well, there are many more couples seeking to adopt babies and there are babies to be adopted.
That's a reality.
But the - but when you look at adopting out of foster care, there's a myth there, and that is that the child is going to cause problems for the family.
And that's not really, generally, the case.
I mean, all of the children can be challenging to parents.
But the - when you adopt a child out of foster care, you're going to know what you're getting into usually.
So it's - that kind of reason for going abroad is based on a myth.
So there people that think, basically, that it will be easier to deal with the system as well as the child if you get one from another country.
There is a reality, too, Tony, to adopting out of foster care and that is that you foster parent a child for a few years and still not be able to adopt him or her.
In the meantime, you've fallen in love with the child, and the - but the child may be returned.
When you're adopting internationally, it has its own challenges, but it - in some ways, it's a little bit more predictable than adopting domestically.
In what way would it be more predictable?
Well, an agency can tell you when in any given a country how much time we're talking about, what the process is with some predictability.
Now, there are some surprises in the international adoption, too, though.
There can be some reactions, some nationalistic reaction within countries opposed to international adoption that can cause a country to shut down or slow down.
Let's talk to Sherry for a moment about her particular experiences.
Sherry, you and your husband have a kid in college and two teenagers, is that right?
Yes.
Now, you're adding to your family by adopting a child from Ethiopia.
So question number one is this: Why did you look outside to U.S.
to adopt to begin with?
And secondly, why Ethiopia in particular?
We looked outside the U.S.
We really enjoy raising children and we feel like we have a lot to offer as a family.
But we wanted to try to make the best impact that we could in terms of humanitarian gesture and humanitarian effort.

When you look at the situation in the pre-emerging and developing countries and you find that there - for instance, there's one doctor for every 35,000 people, and you find that only 11 percent of a population has access to safe water.
And you see that there are children there who need a good home and who could benefit from the potential here in America.
That made it an easy choice.

Ethiopia, specifically, we chose - we were definitely looking for an African country because we are African-American and we want the child to be comfortable in America, you know, racially and also socially.

Ethiopia, in particular, because it's one of the countries that has a cooperative stance, and the agency that we're working with is already there with non-governmental programs to assist the country as well as to do the adoption.
Now, have you had any personal experience with the domestic adoption agencies here?

And secondly, has the process with Ethiopia gone as you would have expected, because Tom just said that on occasion, international adoption can go - he didn't say more smoothly, but that was sort of the implication.
I - we have not had a relationship with a - with domestic adoption, although, we would not rule out foster care and I can comment on that later.
When we decided to go with an international adoption, my husband and I - and we explained to our family it's a tenuous process, although, you know, you do what you can.

It is arduous and it is tenuous and you always have to remember that, although, we have been - we have had a child referred to us, something could happen politically or just within the government agency that administers the adoption process and they could call us tomorrow and say it's all off.
Mm-hmm.
And you - I think you have to go into it with that expectation.

Tom, I want to talk a little bit about what Sherry made reference to with regard to Ethiopia and a sort of political/social consciousness that she and her husband had with regard to where they wanted to adopt and why.
Ethiopia was ranked number five on the list of countries where Americans adopt children.
Haiti ranked number 11.
Why are those countries particularly attractive do you think?
The biggest factor in a country being open to international adoption is simply the policy of the government.
If the government is open to this child welfare program, then adoptions will proceed.
If they're not, they won't.
Because adoption agencies are eager to serve the children.
This is what brings adoption agencies into this line of work.
It's desiring to help children, and they will go where they have a cooperative government.
We are delighted to see the growth in the number of African adoptions.
There are - there are still many - it's still, you know, very small amount compared to the orphan challenge in Africa.
And for that matter, that's true of any country of origin for international adoption.
But Ethiopia has been a country that has grown in its commitment to this child welfare program.
Good to be here, Neal, as always.
But as she said, this can be - this can change in a moment.

Fortunately, every country of origin is - has a double-mindedness towards international adoption because the natural instinct of a country to want to be able to take care of their children themselves.
And to a certain extent, we would - you can respect that.
However, we really have to look at it from the perspective of the child.
Mm-hmm.
